chunk_summary: This section outlines clinic-safe prophylaxis and micronutrient dosing for children, including vitamin A, iron-folic acid (IFA), albendazole, and zinc for diarrhea, with specific age-based dosages and administration guidelines to prevent over-dosing and ensure safety.
learning_objectives: Understand age-specific dosing schedules for vitamin A supplementation in children under 5 years.
Learn the prophylactic iron-folic acid (IFA) supplementation guidelines for different age groups.
Identify contraindications and safety precautions for albendazole administration during mass deworming campaigns.
Understand zinc supplementation protocols for managing acute diarrhea in children.
key_facts: Vitamin A prophylaxis: 9 doses by 5 years, starting at 9 months (100,000 IU) and continuing every 6 months (200,000 IU).

Prophylactic IFA: 6–59 months biweekly syrup (20 mg iron + 100 mcg folic acid); 5–10 years weekly tablet (45 mg iron + 400 mcg folic acid); 10–19 years weekly tablet (60 mg iron + 500 mcg folic acid).
Albendazole dosing: 1–2 years 200 mg; ≥2 years 400 mg; not routine for children under 1 year.
Zinc for acute diarrhea: 2–6 months 10 mg daily; 6 months–5 years 20 mg daily for 14 days, paired with ORS and continued feeding.
Prophylactic IFA should be paused during therapeutic iron treatment to avoid double dosing.
Albendazole should not be administered to acutely febrile or severely ill children; defer dosing to a later visit.
reference_quote: Zinc in acute diarrhea: 2 months to <6 months 10 mg daily; 6 months to 5 years 20 mg daily, for 14 days even if stools stop earlier; pair with ORS, continued feeding/breastfeeding, and clear dehydration danger signs.
document_key_topics: Iron-folic acid prophylaxis for children and adolescents
Zinc supplementation in acute diarrhea management
